title: Houston Fights For Space Shuttle In Unnecessary Discovery Feud
body:
In a battle that has been waged for years, the city of Houston is still fighting to claim one of the retired space shuttles, despite the odds being stacked against it. The Space Center Houston, a leading institution in the city, was one of 29 museums and institutions that applied to receive one of the three decommissioned space orbiters after the space shuttle program was retired by President George W. Bush. However, the Smithsonian Institution has emerged as the clear frontrunner to acquire the coveted spacecraft, leaving Houston's chances of success hanging in the balance.

The space shuttle program, which was a cornerstone of the US space program for over three decades, was officially retired in 2011, after which the National Aeronautics and Space Administration (NASA) announced plans to decommission its fleet of space shuttles. The decision sparked a frenzy of interest among museums and institutions across the country, all of which were eager to get their hands on one of the historic spacecraft. However, the process of selecting the institutions that would ultimately receive the shuttles has been a long and arduous one, with the Smithsonian Institution emerging as the clear favorite to acquire the Discovery space shuttle.

One of the main reasons why the Smithsonian Institution is likely to emerge victorious in this battle is its unparalleled reputation and resources. As one of the world's largest and most respected museums, the Smithsonian has a long history of preserving and showcasing historic artifacts, including spacecraft and other relics of the US space program. The institution's National Air and Space Museum, which is one of its most popular attractions, is already home to a vast collection of historic aircraft and spacecraft, including the Apollo 11 command module and the Wright Brothers' Flyer. The addition of the Discovery space shuttle would be a major coup for the museum, and would cement its position as one of the world's leading institutions for the study and preservation of space exploration history.

Despite the Smithsonian's strong claim to the Discovery space shuttle, the city of Houston is refusing to give up its fight. Space Center Houston, which is the official visitor center of NASA's Johnson Space Center, has been lobbying hard to bring one of the retired space shuttles to the city. The institution argues that Houston's rich history of space exploration, which dates back to the early days of the US space program, makes it an ideal location for one of the historic spacecraft. Additionally, the city's proximity to NASA's Johnson Space Center, where many of the space shuttles were designed, built, and operated, makes it a logical choice for the acquisition of one of the shuttles.

However, the city's chances of success are slim, and the ongoing battle to acquire one of the space shuttles has been criticized by some as a waste of time and resources. With the Smithsonian Institution's reputation and resources, it is likely that the Discovery space shuttle will ultimately end up in its collection. Nevertheless, the city of Houston's determination to bring one of the historic spacecraft to the city is a testament to its commitment to preserving and promoting the history of space exploration. As the US space program continues to evolve and push the boundaries of human knowledge and achievement, the importance of preserving its history and legacy will only continue to grow.

As the battle to acquire the Discovery space shuttle continues, it remains to be seen whether the city of Houston will ultimately emerge victorious. However, regardless of the outcome, the ongoing debate highlights the importance of preserving and promoting the history of space exploration, and the need for institutions and museums to work together to promote the public's understanding and appreciation of this fascinating and complex field. As we look to the future and the next great chapters in the story of space exploration, it is clear that the preservation of our shared cultural heritage will play a vital role in inspiring and educating future generations of scientists, engineers, and explorers.
